self_report.reflection: Substituting t=u^2 gives I(a)=2e^{-2a}\int_0^\infty e^{-a(u-1/u)^2}du. Using the involution u->1/u and adding yields 2J=\int_{-\infty}^\infty e^{-av^2}dv=\sqrt{\pi/a} where v=u-1/u. Thus I(a)=\sqrt{\pi/a}\,e^{-2a}, matching the asserted closed form.
self_report.notes: Standard Putnam 1985 B-5 derivation via t=u^2 substitution and the u->1/u symmetry trick reproduces the claim exactly.
